english: 3. to wander through, traverse, rgyál-k'áms (rgyal khams), the countries, Mil. - 4. to return, go home Sch. - 5. to turn round, twist, mjin ltág-pa (mjin ltag pa) a man's neck, i.e. to choke, to strangle him Glr. Phrases: mgo skór-ba (mgo skor ba), mgo skor byéd-pa (mgo skor byed pa) (W. *co-ce* (*co ce*)) to befool, delude, deceive a person, by intoxication or flattery Glr., also by a flood of words. - *ka kór-wa* (*ka kor wa*) C., *kór-ce* (*kor ce*) W. to make one alter his sentiments, to divert one from a plan etc. - *lan* (*lan*) or *dugs skór-ce* (*dugs skor ce*) to take vengeance W. - *si kór-ce* (*si kor ce*) (v. rtsis (rtsis)) W. to count, calculate. - tsógs-kyi kórlo skór-ba (tsogs kyi korlo skor ba) to arrange the objects of the maṇḍal (q.v.) in a circle n.f. - skor lóg-pa (skor log pa), skor lóg-la gró-ba (skor log la gro ba) to go round the wrong way Mil.; *pé-ra kor-re-lóg tún-ce* (*pe ra kor re log tun ce*) to talk foolishly, to twaddle W. - *lag kór-ce* (*lag kor ce*) the putting a seal under a document which is done by several persons one after another W. Comp. skór-k'añ (skor khang) Glr., prob. = skór-lam (skor lam). - skor-rgyúgs (skor rgyugs) turning the enemy, getting into his rear Mil. - skór-mk'an (skor mkhan), skór-pa (skor pa) a turner Cs. - skor-spyád (skor spyad), skor-śíñ (skor shing) a turner's lathe Cs. - skor-t'íg (skor thig) a pair of compasses. - skor-dbyúg (skor dbyug) a sling, for throwing Sch. - skor-lám (skor lam) 1. the pathway round-about a monastery, used for the holy processions. 2. a veranda surrounding a house. 3. col. also: round-about way. II. 1. the going, moving round, encircling etc. - 2. the way round a thing, = skor-lám (skor lam), in the compounds: náñ-skor (nang skor) the inner, bár-skor (bar skor) the middle, p'yí-skor (phyi skor) the outer roundway, p'yí-skor c'én-po (phyi skor chen po) the outermost. - sá-skor (sa skor) round-about way, by-way.
pf. & ft. bskol (bskol) to boil (vb. act., cf. °kól-ba ('khol ba)).
1. v. skó-ba (sko ba). - 2. Sch.: 'to order', but this is sgó-ba (sgo ba).

1. oar C., Thgy.; skya-léb (skya leb) id.; skya-mjúg (skya mjug) rudder; skya rgyáb-pa (skya rgyab pa) to row Schr. - 2. spatula Schr. - 3. pot-ladle, C. - 4. wall of stone or clay, bár-skya (bar skya), partition-wall, *bhár-kya ca'-pa* (*bhar kya ca' pa*) to make a partition-wall C.
skyá-ga (skya ga) Lt., n. of a bird, Cs.: magpie.

I. vb. 1. pf. bskyas (bskyas), ft. bskya (bskya) 1. Lex.: = p'ó-ba (pho ba) to change place, cf. skyas (skyas). - 2. to carry, convey to a place (a quantity of stones, wood, water etc.) W., v. skyéd-pa (skyed pa). - 3. Sch. to swim (?) II. sbst. 1. kettle Sch. - 2. prob. = skya (skya) 1.

Ssk. पाण्डर and पाण्डु, whitish gray, yellowish-white; *skya cág-ce* (*skya cag ce*) to fry or toast a thing so that its whitish colour turns partially into brown Ld.; mi skya (mi skya) one clothed in light-gray, (not in red or yellow, as monks are), a layman; sño-skya (sngo skya) light-blue, ljañ-skyá (ljang skya) light-green, and so of the other colours; therefore ser-skya (ser skya) ought to denote light-yellow, but it is also used as an equivalent of कपिल, n. of a saint, (Ser-skyai-groñ (ser skyai grong) = Kapilavastu, an ancient city in Oude, and Buddha's birth-place); originally: 'monkey-coloured', tawny, lto-skyá (lto skya) 'pale' i.e. poor, insipid, miserable food Mil.nt. Comp. *kya-ko-ré* (*kya ko re*), *kya-te-ré* (*kya te re*) pale, white C. - skya-skyá (skya skya) id. Sch. - skya-nár (skya nar), पाटलि n. of a flower, Bignonia graveolens; Skya-nár-gyi-bu (skya nar gyi bu) n. of a city of Old-India Pāṭaliputra, now Patna. - skya-snár (skya snar) acc. to Stg. the colour of the skin of the Indians, brown. - skya-rbáb (skya rbab) Cs.: a kind of dropsy, Sch.: a grayish oedematic swelling; skya-rbab-skráñs (skya rbab skrangs) Lex. - *skya-már* (*skya mar*) fresh (i.e. not melted) butter W. - *skya 'ód* (*skya 'od*) W., skya-réñs (skya rengs) morning-twilight, dawn. - skya-lám (skya lam) = skyá-bo (skya bo) Thgy., C. - skya-séñ (skya seng) 1. n. of a tree. 2. translation of Pāndu, skya-séñ-gi bu (skya seng gi bu) a Pandava. - skya-sér (skya ser) 1. Sch.: tawny, cf. ser-skya (ser skya). 2. 'white and yellow' viz.: men, lay-men and priests Mil.nt.
1. = rkyág-pa (rkyag pa). - 2. pf. bskyags (bskyags), ft. bskyag (bskyag), imp. skyog (skyog) to <
